section.title: Wealth, Poverty, and Instruction
section.sectionRationale: Continuing the first Solomonic collection, 10:1 to 22:16, this chapter clusters proverbs on integrity versus riches gained by folly, the fragile loyalty that money buys, the discipline of children, and the LORD's purpose standing firm over every human plan.
section.thesis: Integrity outweighs riches, kindness to the poor is a loan to the LORD Himself, and though many plans fill a man's heart, it is the purpose of the LORD that stands, so wisdom means accepting instruction and fearing Him rather than chasing wealth or resenting discipline.
section.frameNote: Six clusters: integrity better than riches or haste; friendship, false witness, and the favor money buys; the LORD's purpose stands; discipline your son while there is hope; the fear of the LORD leads to life; final warnings against sloth, cruelty, and rebellion.

units.label: Integrity better than riches or haste
units.note: the poor who walk with integrity

verses.text: Better a poor man who walks with integrity than a fool whose lips are perverse.
Even zeal is no good without knowledge, and he who hurries his footsteps misses the mark.
A man's own folly subverts his way, yet his heart rages against the LORD.

units.label: Friendship, false witness, and the favor money buys

verses.text: Wealth attracts many friends, but a poor man is deserted by his friend.
A false witness will not go unpunished, and one who utters lies will not escape.
Many seek the favor of the prince, and everyone is a friend of the gift giver.
All the brothers of a poor man hate him, how much more do his friends avoid him! He may pursue them with pleading, but they are nowhere to be found.
He who acquires {{chokmah|wisdom}} loves himself; one who safeguards understanding will find success.
A false witness will not go unpunished, and one who pours out lies will perish.
Luxury is unseemly for a fool, how much worse for a slave to rule over princes!

units.label: The LORD's purpose stands
units.note: patience, kingship, and the fear of the LORD

verses.text: A man's insight gives him patience, and his virtue is to overlook an offense.
A king's rage is like the roar of a lion, but his favor is like dew on the grass.
A foolish son is his father's ruin, and a quarrelsome wife is like a constant dripping.
Houses and wealth are inherited from fathers, but a prudent wife is from the LORD.
Laziness brings on deep sleep, and an idle soul will suffer hunger.
He who keeps a commandment preserves his soul, but he who is careless in his ways will die.
Kindness to the poor is a loan to the LORD, and He will repay the lender.

units.label: Discipline your son while there is hope

verses.text: Discipline your son, for in that there is hope; do not be party to his death.
A man of great anger must pay the penalty; if you rescue him, you will have to do so again.
Listen to counsel and accept discipline, that you may be wise the rest of your days.

units.label: The fear of the LORD leads to life
units.note: the LORD's purpose over man's plans

verses.text: Many plans are in a man's heart, but the purpose of the LORD will prevail.
The desire of a man is loving devotion; better to be poor than a liar.
The {{yirah|fear}} of the LORD leads to life, that one may rest content, without visitation from harm.
The slacker buries his hand in the dish; he will not even bring it back to his mouth.
Strike a mocker, and the simple will beware; rebuke the discerning man, and he will gain knowledge.
He who assaults his father or evicts his mother is a son who brings shame and disgrace.
If you cease to hear instruction, my son, you will stray from the words of knowledge.
A corrupt witness mocks justice, and a wicked mouth swallows iniquity.
Judgments are prepared for mockers, and beatings for the backs of fools.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, the skill of living rightly before God and others. Verse 8 says whoever acquires it 'loves himself,' meaning true self-love is not indulgence but the pursuit of wisdom, since wisdom preserves the very soul that pursues it.

yirah.gloss: Fear, reverent awe. Verse 23 says this fear of the LORD leads to life and lets a person rest content, untouched by real harm, the same fear that Proverbs 1:7 calls the beginning of knowledge.

basic.context: This chapter weighs integrity against wealth gained by folly or flattery, exposes how money buys fickle friendship while poverty drives even family away, and calls fathers to discipline their sons while there is hope. It closes with the great truth that though people make many plans, it is the LORD's purpose that finally prevails.
basic.hermeneutics: These proverbs describe patterns of life, not ironclad guarantees; not every poor person has integrity and not every rich person lacks it, but the chapter consistently ranks character above cash and warns that wealth's friendships are often fickle while the LORD's favor toward the faithful is not.
basic.originalLanguages: Chokmah, wisdom, verse 8, is something a person acquires for the sake of his own soul, since pursuing it is true self-love, not selfishness. Yirah, the fear of the LORD, verse 23, leads to life and rest, echoing the refrain of Proverbs that reverence for God, not self-reliance, is the beginning of true living.
basic.typology: Kindness to the poor as a loan to the LORD, verse 17, anticipates Christ's identification with the poor and needy, Matthew 25:40, where kindness shown to the least is kindness shown to Him. The father's discipline of a son while there is hope, verse 18, foreshadows the discipline of the Father who disciplines those He loves, Hebrews 12:6.
basic.christ: Verse 17 says kindness to the poor is a loan to the LORD, who will repay the lender, a truth Christ makes explicit when He says whatever is done for the least of His brothers is done for Him, Matthew 25:40. And the chapter's confidence that the LORD's purpose will prevail over every human plan, verse 21, is the same confidence the gospel rests on, that God's redemptive plan in Christ cannot be overturned by human scheming, Acts 4:27 to 28.
basic.god: God is the true creditor behind every act of mercy to the poor, verse 17, and the sovereign whose purpose stands firm no matter how many plans fill the human heart, verse 21. He disciplines those He loves the way a wise father disciplines a son while there is hope, verse 18, aiming always at life, not despair.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 25:34-40 shows Christ identifying with the poor, fulfilling the loan to the LORD of 19:17. Hebrews 12:5-11 develops fatherly discipline for our good. Acts 4:27-28 shows the LORD's purpose prevailing even through human plots. James 1:19-20 echoes the patience and slowness to anger commended in 19:11.
basic.oneStory: Proverbs 19 calls God's people to integrity over wealth, mercy to the poor, patient discipline of children, and trust that the LORD's purpose will prevail over every human scheme. All of this comes to fullest expression in Christ, who identified with the poor, endured the Father's purpose through the cross, and now disciplines His people toward life.

context.literary: Chapter 19 continues the loosely thematic clusters of the first Solomonic collection, weaving together sayings on integrity, wealth's fickle friendships, family discipline, and the LORD's overruling purpose, ending with sober warnings against sloth and rebellion.
context.historical: In an agrarian, patronage based society, wealth genuinely bought friendship and influence, verses 4 and 6, while the poor could be abandoned even by brothers, verse 7; these proverbs confronted a real social dynamic still recognizable today.
context.canonical: Verse 21's confidence that the LORD's purpose prevails despite many human plans echoes Joseph's words in Genesis 50:20 and anticipates the New Testament's confidence that God works all things according to the purpose of His will, Ephesians 1:11.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To commend integrity over riches gained by folly, expose the fickleness of wealth-bought friendship, urge patient discipline of children, and ground all of life in confidence that the LORD's purpose, not human planning, finally prevails.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses like 17, 18, and 20 prescribe conduct directly, be kind to the poor, discipline your son, accept counsel, while verses like 4, 6, and 7 describe an often ugly social reality, wealth buys friends and poverty drives even family away. The chapter's purpose is not to endorse that reality but to expose it so the reader chooses integrity instead.

debates.question: Does verse 18, 'discipline your son... do not be party to his death,' commend harsh or even physical punishment as necessary?

views.label: It calls for firm, possibly physical correction
views.case: Proverbs elsewhere speaks of the rod, 13:24 and 23:13-14, and this verse's urgency, 'do not be party to his death,' suggests real consequences follow a failure to correct.

views.label: It calls for timely, hope-filled correction, with method left open
views.case: The verse's emphasis falls on 'while there is hope' and avoiding a passive parenting that lets a child destroy himself; the text specifies urgency and purpose, not a particular method, and later revelation, Ephesians 6:4, tempers all discipline with love that does not provoke a child to anger.

debates.bearing: Read together with the rest of Scripture, the verse's core point is urgency and hope, correction offered in time, not a mandate for any single disciplinary method; parents are called to discipline that aims at life, not despair.

study.originalLanguages: Chokmah, wisdom, verse 8, is framed as something a person acquires 'for himself,' meaning wisdom's pursuit is the truest form of self-care. Yirah, fear of the LORD, verse 23, leads to life and untroubled rest, tying this chapter back to the book's opening refrain that reverence for God, not self-sufficiency, is where real security begins.

typology.type: Kindness to the poor as a loan to the LORD, 19:17
typology.fulfillment: Christ's identification with the poor and needy, Matthew 25:34-40.

typology.type: Fatherly discipline while there is hope, 19:18
typology.fulfillment: The discipline of the heavenly Father toward those He loves, Hebrews 12:5-11.

study.christ: Verse 17's claim that kindness to the poor is a loan to the LORD becomes explicit in Christ's own words, that whatever is done for the least of His brothers is done for Him, Matthew 25:40, so mercy to the poor is never wasted but repaid by God Himself. And where the chapter insists the LORD's purpose will prevail over every human plan, verse 21, the gospel shows this most starkly: wicked hands crucified Jesus, yet they carried out exactly what God's plan had decided beforehand, Acts 4:27 to 28, so that human sin and divine purpose met at the cross to bring salvation.
study.god: God stands behind every kindness shown to the poor as its true recipient and rewarder, verse 17, and His purpose cannot be thwarted by the many competing plans of the human heart, verse 21. He is also a Father who disciplines, urging correction while there is still hope, verse 18, because His aim in discipline, like a wise father's, is always life and restoration, not destruction.

crossReferences.note: Christ identifies with the poor and needy, fulfilling the loan to the LORD of Proverbs 19:17.
The Lord disciplines those He loves for their good, echoing the urgency of Proverbs 19:18.
God's purpose prevailed even through the plots of wicked men, embodying Proverbs 19:21.
God works all things according to the purpose of His will, the New Testament's fuller statement of Proverbs 19:21.

study.oneStory: Proverbs 19 calls for integrity over wealth, mercy to the poor, patient and hopeful discipline, and trust that the LORD's purpose stands over every human plan. Every strand finds its center in Christ, who identified with the poor, was disciplined and crucified according to God's determined purpose, and now leads His people toward the life that reverent fear of the LORD promises.

quiz.q: According to verse 1, what is better than a fool with perverse lips?

options.text: A poor man who walks with integrity.
options.note: Integrity outweighs both wealth and eloquence when character is corrupt.

options.text: A rich man with perverse lips.
options.note: This is the very thing the verse ranks below integrity.

options.text: A wealthy man of few words.
options.note: Wealth is not the point of comparison in this verse; integrity versus folly is.

options.text: A poor man who is also foolish.
options.note: The verse specifically praises poverty paired with integrity, not with foolishness.

quiz.q: What does verse 17 say about kindness to the poor?

options.text: It is a loan to the LORD, and He will repay the lender.
options.note: Mercy to the poor is treated as a debt God Himself takes on and repays.

options.text: It is wasted generosity with no lasting effect.
options.note: The verse promises repayment from the LORD, not futility.

options.text: It obligates the poor person to repay the giver directly.
options.note: The repayment comes from the LORD, not from the poor person helped.

options.text: It is commendable but carries no promise attached.
options.note: The verse explicitly attaches a promise, that the LORD will repay.

quiz.q: What does verse 21 declare about human plans and the LORD's purpose?

options.text: Many plans are in a man's heart, but the purpose of the LORD will prevail.
options.note: Human planning is real but subordinate to God's overruling purpose.

options.text: Human plans always fail completely.
options.note: The verse contrasts plans with the LORD's prevailing purpose, not declaring all plans failures outright.

options.text: The LORD has no purpose of His own, only reacting to human plans.
options.note: This is the opposite; the LORD's purpose is what finally prevails.

options.text: Human plans and the LORD's purpose are always identical.
options.note: The verse implies real tension between the two, resolved by the LORD's purpose prevailing, not by automatic identity.

quiz.q: What does verse 18 urge concerning a son?

options.text: Discipline him while there is hope, and do not be party to his death.
options.note: Timely, hope-filled correction is urged with real urgency.

options.text: Wait until adulthood before offering any correction.
options.note: The verse's urgency, 'while there is hope,' argues against delay.

options.text: Discipline is optional if the son seems naturally good.
options.note: The verse gives no such exception; the call to discipline is general.

options.text: Discipline should be applied only occasionally.
options.note: The verse's urgency suggests sustained, timely correction, not mere occasional action.

quiz.q: What does verse 23 say the fear of the LORD leads to?

options.text: Life, and rest without harm.
options.note: Reverent fear of God is tied to genuine security and life.

options.text: Constant anxiety and unrest.
options.note: This is the opposite of the rest the verse promises.

options.text: Wealth and social status.
options.note: The verse's promise is life and rest, not material wealth or status.

options.text: A general sense of caution, without real security.
options.note: The verse promises more than caution; it promises life and rest from harm.

questions: Verse 1 prizes integrity over cleverness or wealth. Where are you tempted to cut corners on character to get ahead?
Wealth attracts many friends, but poverty scatters even family, verses 4 and 7. Has your own capacity for friendship been shaped more by what people can offer you than by love?
Kindness to the poor is called a loan to the LORD, verse 17. What would change in your giving if you truly believed God Himself was the one repaying it?
Many plans fill the human heart, but the LORD's purpose prevails, verse 21. What plan are you gripping tightly that you need to hold with an open hand before God?
Verse 20 calls us to listen to counsel and accept discipline for a lifetime of wisdom. Whose correction have you been resisting that you need to receive humbly this week?

translationNotes.note: 'Luxury is unseemly for a fool' pictures social order upended, a related image to a slave ruling over princes, both describing things that violate a fitting, expected order.
'Do not be party to his death' can also be rendered 'do not set your heart on his destruction,' meaning withholding correction is a kind of complicity in the child's ruin.
The image of a slacker burying his hand in the dish yet failing to bring it to his mouth repeats at 26:15, a vivid caricature of paralyzing laziness.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 19. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
